metadata.file_name: 123. Schipper et al. (2025). Identifying students’ solution strategies in digital mathematics assessment using log data.pdf
metadata.title: Identifying students’ solution strategies in digital mathematics assessment using log data

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses French national low-stakes assessment CEDRE (2019) administered in ninth grade (troisième) in both public and private schools, with an analytic subset defined by log-data cleaning for the single “Product Equation” item. The authors report removing students who did not interact at all, those with missing score/estimated ability/response time, and those with technical malfunctions in response-time registration, resulting in 802 students. The manuscript does not mention applying CEDRE complex survey weights or replicate-weight procedures (e.g., BRR/jackknife), and the analysis is based on finite mixture modeling of log-derived variables plus group comparisons of estimated mathematics ability. Because no weighting variables or survey-design adjustments are described, the analysis is treated as unweighted; the paper also does not discuss why weights were omitted.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: Plausible values are not applicable because the dependent outcome used to relate solution-strategy classes to mathematical ability is an estimated ability score derived from a 2PL model using Warm’s Weighted Likelihood Estimation (WLE), not PV-based achievement scores. Missing data handling is implemented via case removal: students with no registered score, estimated ability, or response time were removed, and additional removals were made for lack of interaction and a technical malfunction in response-time registration. Therefore, the paper does not require PV-specific combining rules.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Used CEDRE 2019 ninth-grade digital assessment log data for the single “Product Equation” item. Excluded students who did not interact with the assessment environment at all; excluded students for whom no score, estimated ability, or response time was registered; removed five additional students due to a technical malfunction in the registration of their response times. Further cleaned item-level log interactions by removing construction events (typing longer character sequences) and deletion events (keystrokes registered during deletion), and then constructed seven log-derived variables for finite mixture clustering.

main_findings.dataset_used: CEDRE 2019 Grade 9 (troisième) Digital Mathematics — “Product Equation” item (France)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Not reported (model fit via BIC/AIC for 2–6 classes; selected 5-class model). Class sizes: 178, 101, 228, 177, 118.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using CEDRE 2019 Grade 9 digital mathematics data for the “Product Equation” item, the study leveraged entered values (x and -2/3), interaction counts, and time-based log aggregates to identify five distinct solution-strategy classes, finding that students differed meaningfully in in-assessment behavior (e.g., absent, algebraic, pragmatic, trial-and-error, disengaged). This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: The study analyzes French CEDRE 2019 ninth-grade digital mathematics log data for a single “Product Equation” item, after cleaning log interactions and excluding students with missing score/ability/response-time information, yielding 802 students. It identifies five distinct student solution-strategy classes using a finite mixture model on seven log-derived variables (entered values, interaction counts, and time-based aggregates), selecting the 5-class solution based on interpretability and model fit (BIC/AIC across 2–6 classes). The classes differ in estimated mathematical ability (WLE theta) derived from the full assessment: the disengaged class (class 5) shows the lowest mean ability and is significantly lower than classes 1–4 (p < 0.001 after Bonferroni; effect sizes around d ≈ -0.86 to -1.11). The paper does not report predictive ML performance metrics (e.g., accuracy/AUC) because the primary goal is clustering/profiling rather than supervised prediction, and it does not describe the use of survey weights or replicate-weight methods.
